PLO 2 — RAT Objective

Describe the function of proto-oncogenes, oncogenes, tumor suppressor genes, and DNA repair genes

Know the difference between gain-of-function (oncogene) and loss-of-function (TSG), how many allele hits are needed, and whether they are typically inherited.

  • ✓
    Proto-oncogene → normal gene; becomes oncogene via gain-of-function mutation or overexpression
  • ✓
    Oncogene → 1 mutant allele sufficient (dominant); constitutively promotes proliferation; rarely inherited
  • ✓
    Tumor suppressor gene (TSG) → loss-of-function; both alleles needed (recessive); commonly inherited
  • ✓
    DNA repair genes → subset of TSGs (caretakers); inactivation causes mutator phenotype → faster cancer progression
  • ✓
    Analogy: Oncogene = stuck gas pedal; TSG = cut brake line
PLO 2
LO 2

Gene Classes in Cancer

Proto-Oncogenes, Oncogenes, Tumor Suppressor Genes & DNA Repair Genes

Cancer results from dysregulation of positive and negative proliferation signals. Two fundamental categories of genes drive this process.

Slide 8 — Positive vs. Negative Proliferation Signals

🚗💨

Proto-Oncogenes → Oncogenes

A proto-oncogene is a normal gene involved in regulating cell growth (e.g., RAS, EGFR). When mutated or overexpressed, it becomes an oncogene — continuously sending "grow!" signals even without external stimulus.

  • Mutation type: Gain-of-function (dominant)
  • Only 1 mutant allele needed
  • Rarely inherited (disrupts embryogenesis)
  • Functions: growth factors, receptors, signal transducers, TFs, positive cell-cycle regulators
🛑

Tumor Suppressor Genes (TSGs)

TSGs apply the brakes on cell proliferation. They form a network of checkpoints — cell cycle inhibitors, DNA repair genes, apoptosis-inducers, and transcription factors.

  • Mutation type: Loss-of-function (generally recessive)
  • Both alleles must be inactivated ("two hits")
  • Commonly inherited → increased cancer risk
  • Examples: RB1, p53, APC, BRCA1/2

Slide 10 — Oncogenes: Gain-of-Function Mutations Driving Uncontrolled Growth

DNA Repair Genes

DNA repair genes are a subset of tumor suppressor genes called caretakers. They maintain the integrity of the genome. When mutated:

  • Damaged DNA is no longer fixed → mutations accumulate faster
  • Accelerates the multi-step process of carcinogenesis
  • Examples: BRCA1/2 (breast/ovarian cancer), mismatch repair genes (Lynch syndrome)
🔑 Key Analogy

Think of cancer like a car with the gas pedal stuck down (oncogene) and the brakes cut (TSG). You need both failure modes to achieve uncontrolled speed — which is why 6–8 driver mutations are required for a fully malignant tumor.

Classifying Genes: Oncogene vs. TSG

FeatureOncogeneTumor Suppressor Gene
Normal gene nameProto-oncogeneTumor suppressor gene
Mutation typeGain of functionLoss of function
Alleles needed1 (dominant)2 (recessive, usually)
Effect on growth↑ Accelerates↓ Removes brake
Inherited?RarelyCommonly
ExamplesRAS, EGFR, BCL-2, Cyclin DRB1, TP53, APC, BRCA1, TSC2

Q: Patient X presents with B-cell lymphoma showing increased BCL-2 expression. BCL-2 normally inhibits apoptosis. What type of gene is BCL-2 in these cells?

Answer

Oncogene. Overexpression = gain-of-function. BCL-2 allows cells to evade apoptosis, driving tumor survival. In normal cells it is a proto-oncogene.

Q: RB1 normally suppresses entry into G1 phase. Both alleles are mutated in a retinoblastoma tumor. What type of gene is RB1, and how do mutations promote growth?

Answer

Tumor suppressor gene. RB1 inhibits E2F. When both alleles are lost (loss-of-function), E2F is constitutively active → drives G1 entry → uncontrolled proliferation.

PLO 1 — RAT Objective

Describe the role of p53 in repairing DNA damage and mechanism of cell cycle regulation

Know p53's trigger, how it arrests the cell cycle, how it promotes repair or apoptosis, and why its loss is so catastrophic for cancer suppression.

  • ✓
    p53 = "Guardian of the genome" — tumor suppressor gene (TP53); regulates hundreds of genes
  • ✓
    Triggered by: DNA strand breaks, UV damage, hypoxia, oncogene activation → ATM/ATR phosphorylate p53 → MDM2 can't degrade it → p53 accumulates
  • ✓
    Cell cycle arrest: Induces p21 → inhibits CDK–Cyclin complexes → blocks G1/S and G2/M transitions
  • ✓
    DNA repair: Upregulates GADD45 and other repair genes → time to fix damage before replication
  • ✓
    Apoptosis (if irreparable): Activates BAX, PUMA, NOXA → intrinsic apoptotic pathway → cytochrome c → caspases → cell death
  • ✓
    Mutated in >50% of all human tumors
  • ✓
    Li-Fraumeni syndrome: Inherited one mutant TP53 allele → 25× increased cancer risk by age 50
PLO 1

Tumor Suppressor

p53 — "Guardian of the Genome"

Slide 25 — p53: Regulator of Cell Cycle & DNA Damage Response

p53 is the most important tumor suppressor gene in human cancer, mutated in over 50% of all human tumors. It is encoded by TP53 and regulates hundreds of downstream genes.

🔍

DNA Damage Sensor

Activated by DNA breaks, UV, hypoxia, oncogene stress. ATM/ATR kinases phosphorylate p53 → prevents MDM2 degradation → p53 accumulates.

⏸️

Cell Cycle Arrest

Induces p21 (CDKN1A) → inhibits CDK–Cyclin complexes → halts G1/S and G2/M checkpoints.

🔧

DNA Repair

Upregulates GADD45 and other repair genes — gives the cell time to fix damage before replication resumes.

💀

Apoptosis Induction

If damage is irreparable, p53 activates BAX, PUMA, NOXA → intrinsic apoptotic pathway → programs the cell to die.

Mechanism of Cell Cycle Regulation

  1. DNA damage detected — ATM/ATR kinases phosphorylate p53, blocking MDM2 binding → p53 protein stabilizes and accumulates in nucleus.
  2. p21 induced — p53 transcriptionally activates CDKN1A (p21), a universal CDK inhibitor → blocks G1→S (and G2→M) transition → cell cycle arrest.
  3. Repair window — DNA repair machinery (GADD45, etc.) has time to fix lesions. If successful, MDM2 resumes p53 degradation, cycle continues.
  4. Apoptosis if irreparable — p53 activates BAX and other pro-apoptotic genes → mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization → cytochrome c release → caspase cascade → cell death.
🔑 Why p53 Loss Is So Devastating

Without p53, cells with damaged DNA skip arrest, bypass repair, and evade apoptosis — accelerating the accumulation of all other cancer-driving mutations. It is effectively the last line of defense for genomic integrity before malignant transformation.

  • In most tumors: both TP53 alleles mutated somatically
  • Li-Fraumeni Syndrome: Germline one mutant TP53 allele → 25× cancer risk by age 50 (sarcomas, brain, breast, leukemia) → classified as dominantly inherited predisposition
🐘 Fun Fact: Elephants & Cancer Resistance

Elephants have at least 20 copies (40 alleles) of TP53 — making them extraordinarily cancer-resistant. All 40 alleles would need to be knocked out. This illustrates why gene dosage of tumor suppressors matters so profoundly.

RB1 & the Cell Cycle (Related TSG Mechanism)

Slide 23 — RB1/E2F Pathway: Growth-Inhibitory Signaling

  • No growth signal: RB1 binds E2F → G1 entry blocked
  • Growth signal present: CDKs phosphorylate RB1 → E2F released → S phase entry
  • Both RB1 alleles mutated: E2F constitutively active → unchecked proliferation (Retinoblastoma)
PLO 3 — RAT Objective

Describe the disease mechanism of inherited and sporadic cancers

Know the three categories, how hereditary cancers work mechanistically (germline TSG mutation → single somatic hit → tumor), and why only 5–10% of cancers are truly inherited.

  • ✓
    Inherited (hereditary) cancer: Germline TSG mutation inherited → every cell is already one hit away from cancer → earlier onset, higher risk, may be bilateral/multiple
  • ✓
    Familial cancer: Clusters in families due to shared mutations OR shared environment/lifestyle — not always a clear germline cause
  • ✓
    Sporadic cancer: No family history, no inherited mutation; both hits acquired somatically over a lifetime — 90–95% of all cancers
  • ✓
    Why TSGs, not oncogenes, are inherited: Inheriting an oncogene would cause uncontrolled embryonic proliferation → lethal. One mutant TSG allele is tolerated during development.
  • ✓
    Gatekeepers vs. Caretakers: Gatekeepers = cell cycle checkpoints (RB1, p53, APC); Caretakers = DNA repair (BRCA1/2, mismatch repair)
  • ✓
    Only 5–10% of all cancers stem from inherited mutations
PLO 3
LO 3

Disease Mechanism

Inherited, Familial & Sporadic Cancers

Slide 13 — Oncogenes vs. TSGs in Hereditary Cancer Risk

TypeDefinitionUnderlying MechanismPrevalence
Inherited (Hereditary)Cancer syndrome caused by germline TSG mutation passed parent → childGermline first hit inherited in every cell; somatic second hit causes tumor5–10%
FamilialCancer clusters in families more than expected by chanceMay be TSG mutations OR shared environment/lifestyleVariable
SporadicNo family history; no inherited predisposing mutationBoth hits acquired somatically over a lifetime~90–95%

Two-Hit Mechanism (Foundation of Hereditary Cancer)

Slide 14 — Knudson's Two-Hit Hypothesis

Slide 15 — Why Inherited TSG Mutations Increase Cancer Risk (RB1)

Non-Hereditary (Sporadic)

Born with: ++ →Hit 1 (rare) m+ →Hit 2 (rare) mm Cancer

Requires two independent rare events → less common, later onset

Hereditary

Born with: m+ High Risk →Only 1 hit needed mm Cancer

First hit inherited in every cell → only one rare event away → earlier onset, higher probability

Gatekeepers vs. Caretakers

🚪 Gatekeepers

Directly regulate the cell cycle. Loss removes checkpoint control.

  • RB1 (retinoblastoma)
  • TP53
  • APC (colon cancer)
  • TSC2 (tuberous sclerosis)

🔧 Caretakers

Maintain genomic integrity. Loss → mutator phenotype → faster accumulation of all mutations.

  • BRCA1/BRCA2
  • MLH1, MSH2 (Lynch)
  • XPC (Xeroderma pigmentosum)
🔑 Why TSGs (Not Oncogenes) Are Inherited

Inheriting an oncogene would drive unrestricted embryonic cell proliferation → incompatible with normal development → lethal. Inheriting one mutant TSG allele has no effect during development (the other allele compensates), so the mutation is transmitted. This is why nearly all hereditary cancer syndromes involve TSG mutations.

✅ Clinical Pearl

Suspect hereditary cancer syndrome when a patient presents with: multiple tumors, bilateral tumors, cancer at unusually young age, or multiple affected family members. Genetic counseling + germline testing are indicated — findings affect surveillance for the whole family.

LO 1

Pathogenesis

The Multistep Model of Cancer

Cancer does not arise from a single mutation. It is a sequential accumulation of 6–8 driver mutations over time, beginning in a single cell that undergoes clonal expansion.

Slide 4 — Cancer: Imbalance Between Cell Growth and Cell Death

Slide 5 — Multistep Carcinogenesis: Single Cell → Invasive Heterogeneous Tumor

Core Concept: Clonal Expansion & Heterogeneity

A tumor begins from one precursor cell. As daughter cells replicate, they independently accumulate different additional mutations, creating a heterogeneous tumor mass with multiple subclones.

Clinical implication: Tumor heterogeneity is why single chemotherapy agents often fail — resistant subclones survive and repopulate the tumor.

  1. Driver mutation #1 — Single normal cell acquires oncogene activation. Cell gains proliferative advantage.
  2. Clonal expansion — Mutant cell produces many copies; a second driver mutation may occur (e.g., TSG loss).
  3. Accumulation of 6–8 mutations — Disable TSGs, activate oncogenes, disable DNA repair, enable apoptosis evasion.
  4. Heterogeneity emerges — Different subclones carry different mutation combinations; some gain invasive ability.
  5. Invasion and metastasis — Malignant transformation complete.
⚠️ Why Chemotherapy Resistance Develops

Chemotherapy eliminates susceptible clones. Resistant subclones with different mutations survive, expand, and repopulate the tumor — often more aggressively. Direct consequence of the multistep model and tumor heterogeneity.

LO 3

Knudson's Hypothesis

The Two-Hit Hypothesis — Deep Dive

Proposed by Alfred Knudson (1971) to explain why TSGs require mutation of both alleles. Already covered in the Inherited Cancer section above — this section adds the Retinoblastoma clinical details.

Retinoblastoma: Classic Example

  • Most common childhood eye cancer — 2% of childhood cancers
  • Hereditary form (40%): bilateral, younger onset; germline RB1 mutation → every retinal cell is one hit away
  • Sporadic form (60%): unilateral, older onset; two independent somatic hits required
  • RB1 sequesters E2F → loss of both alleles → unchecked G1 entry
🔑 Why TSGs Are the Basis of Hereditary Cancer

Inheriting a mutant oncogene is lethal to embryos. Inheriting one mutant TSG allele is tolerated — cells still have one functional copy. This is why nearly all inherited cancer predispositions involve TSG mutations. The inherited allele provides the "first hit," making the second somatic hit statistically much more likely to occur.

LO 4

Malignant Transformation

Six Hallmarks of Cancer

Slide 19 — Essential Steps in Malignant Transformation (Hanahan & Weinberg)

1

Self-Sufficiency in Growth Signals

Autocrine loops, constitutively active receptors, locked-on RAS, fusion oncoproteins (BCR-ABL). No external signal needed.

2

Insensitivity to Growth-Inhibitory Signals

Loss of RB1 releases E2F. Loss of p53 removes DNA damage checkpoint. Cells ignore "stop" signals.

3

Evasion of Apoptosis

BCL-2 overexpression in B-cell lymphomas. Damaged cells survive and accumulate further mutations.

4

Limitless Replicative Potential

Telomerase upregulated in 85–95% of cancers → maintains telomere length → replicative immortality.

5

Sustained Angiogenesis

Tumors >1–2 mm secrete VEGF/bFGF → new blood vessels. Anti-VEGF therapy (bevacizumab) targets this.

6

Invasion & Metastasis

MMPs degrade ECM; cells breach basement membrane (intravasation), circulate, extravasate, colonize distant sites.

Slide 21 — RAS Mutations: Constitutive Proliferation (15–20% of tumors)

Slide 22 — BCR-ABL / Philadelphia Chromosome → Gleevec Targeted Therapy

✅ BCR-ABL Clinical Pearl

t(9;22) fuses BCR with ABL → constitutively active tyrosine kinase → CML/ALL. Imatinib (Gleevec) specifically inhibits ABL kinase — first targeted "magic bullet," prototype of personalized oncology.

Slide 27 — Intrinsic & Extrinsic Apoptosis; BCL-2 Overexpression

Slide 28 — Telomere Shortening → Senescence vs. Telomerase → Immortality

Slide 29 — VEGF-Driven Tumor Angiogenesis

Slide 30 — Metastasis Steps: Intravasation → Circulation → Extravasation

Intravasation vs. Extravasation

Intravasation — Tumor cells secrete collagenases/MMPs, degrade basement membrane, invade into blood/lymph vessels.

Extravasation — Circulating tumor cells exit vessels at distant sites, evade immune surveillance, establish metastases.

LO 5

Diagnostic Technology

Cytogenetic & DNA Technologies

🔬 1. Karyotyping

Slide 37 — Karyotyping Protocol

  • Requires living, dividing cells; arrested at metaphase for condensed chromosomes
  • Reveals: aneuploidy, large deletions, duplications, translocations
  • Limitation: ~5–10 Mb resolution; cannot detect point mutations

🌈 2. FISH & Spectral Karyotyping (SKY)

Slide 38 — FISH/SKY: Each Chromosome Painted a Unique Color

  • Fluorescent probes "paint" each chromosome a distinct color → translocations appear as color-mixed chromosomes
  • FISH can also target individual loci (e.g., HER2 amplification, BCR-ABL fusion)

Slide 39 — Chromosomal Abnormalities Identifiable on Karyotype

AbnormalityKaryotype FindingExample
Loss of chromosomeOnly 1 copy of a pairMonosomy 7 in AML
Gain of chromosome3 copiesTrisomy 8 in AML
DeletionShortened chromosome / missing banddel(17p) — TP53
DuplicationExtra band materialHER2 amplification
TranslocationSegment fused to different chromosome (different color in SKY)t(9;22) Philadelphia chr.

🧬 3. DNA Sequencing & 4. Gene Expression Analysis

  • DNA sequencing (NGS): Identifies point mutations, indels, copy number changes, fusions; used for lung cancer panels (EGFR, KRAS, ALK, ROS1, RET)
  • Gene expression (RNA): Measures transcript levels → how much a gene is being expressed; used in MammaPrint 70-gene assay
  • Protein analysis: IHC, Western blot, ELISA → confirms protein presence and activity
LO 6

Individualized Treatment

Genotype-Guided Personalized Cancer Treatment

Slide 44 — Lung Cancer Targeted Gene Panel

Slide 45 — EGFR TKIs: Matching Mutation to Drug

  • EGFR-mutant NSCLC: EGFR TKIs (erlotinib, gefitinib, osimertinib)
  • ALK rearrangements: ALK inhibitors (crizotinib, alectinib)
  • BCR-ABL (CML): Imatinib (Gleevec) — prototype targeted therapy

MammaPrint 70-Gene Assay (Breast Cancer)

Slide 46 — MammaPrint: Clinical Risk vs. Genomic Risk (MINDACT Trial)

Clinical Risk vs. Genomic Risk
TypeBased OnExample
Clinical RiskTumor size/grade, LN status, age, menopausal statusLarge tumor + LN+ + premenopausal = High clinical risk
Genomic Risk70-gene expression profile of tumorLow-risk signature despite high clinical risk
  • MINDACT trial: high clinical risk + low genomic risk patients → 94.7% metastasis-free survival at 5 years without chemotherapy
  • Difference vs. chemo arm: only 1.5 percentage points
  • ~25% of patients spared unnecessary chemotherapy
  • MammaPrint provides genomic risk information, not clinical risk

Q: A lung cancer patient has EGFR exon 19 deletion. A second patient has wild-type EGFR but an ALK rearrangement. What treatments are indicated?

Answer

Patient 1: EGFR TKI (e.g., osimertinib). Patient 2: ALK inhibitor (e.g., crizotinib). Each is ineffective in the other's mutation profile — personalized medicine based on tumor genotype.
